lesson_contract.objective: Practice adaptable responses for price, timing, trust, competition, and decision-maker concerns.
objective_points: Select a script pattern that matches the objection
Adapt wording without changing its intent
Know when to pause and seek guidance
lesson_contract.transition_spoken_text: Take the Objection Scripts Playbook checkpoint next. Then move into Trust, Complexity, and Frequently Asked Questions.

required_teaching_beats.description: Use listen, acknowledge, ask, and redirect as an adaptable practice pattern.
Explore price expectations and what the requested number needs to accomplish.
Compare other offers and sale paths without attacking them.
Clarify what must happen before the seller can decide.
Offer verifiable information and respect outside review.
Clarify repairs, inspections, occupants, and property facts.
Collect facts and route legal, loan, title, or credit questions for qualified review.
Treat special structures, occupancy, price, timing, and costs as written terms subject to review.
Pause when a question crosses authority or professional-advice boundaries.
The close directs the learner to the checkpoint and next lesson.

required_examples.description: Seller wants more money.
Seller asks BMH to name a price first.
Seller may list with an agent.
Seller is talking with other investors.
Seller needs to speak with a spouse.
Seller asks who the company is.
Seller cannot sell now and needs time.
Seller asks for proof of funds.
Seller asks how BMH obtained the number.
Seller wants an attorney to review documents.
Seller may repair the property instead.
Seller is too busy to continue.
Seller is running a highest-and-best process.
Seller says the situation is handled.
Seller may rent the property.
Seller wants a walkthrough before naming expectations.
Seller asks why BMH would not buy the property itself.
Seller asks whether the agreement may be assigned.
Seller wants to avoid repairs and inspections.
Seller does not know the loan payoff.
Seller has occupants and does not know how to proceed.
Seller believes the debt may exceed the available proceeds.
Seller reports foreclosure pressure and a deadline.
Seller needs a particular net amount.
Seller needs to remain after the transaction.
Seller fears a later price change after inspection.
Seller distrusts a non-cash structure.
Seller wants privacy.
Seller wants cash without contingencies.
Seller requests a written offer without discussion.
Seller needs to think.
Seller says they are not interested.

forbidden_language_removals.exact_source_language: If I can guarantee your net
forbidden_language_removals.replacement_rule: Ask which written option the seller wants reviewed. Never promise proceeds.

forbidden_language_removals.exact_source_language: you get a clean, certain outcome without games
forbidden_language_removals.replacement_rule: Describe the review process without promising an outcome.

forbidden_language_removals.exact_source_language: your price, timeline, and certainty don't change
forbidden_language_removals.replacement_rule: State that final terms depend on the written agreement and review.

forbidden_language_removals.exact_source_language: I can bring the loan current, stop the sale, and take over payments so you can breathe again and rebuild your credit
forbidden_language_removals.replacement_rule: Pause, avoid legal or credit promises, and route the facts for qualified review.

forbidden_language_removals.exact_source_language: I can close, fund you, and let you stay
forbidden_language_removals.replacement_rule: Discuss occupancy only as a written option subject to approval.

scenes.title: Practice patterns instead of promises
scenes.spoken_text: Objections are easier to handle when you stop hunting for a perfect comeback. Listen to the words, acknowledge the concern, ask what sits underneath it, and redirect toward a responsible next step. The responses in this lesson are practice patterns. Adapt them to the facts. Do not state that a price, date, structure, legal result, credit result, or exception is available until the current written process confirms it.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea in a direct medium shot
visual_plan.editor_note: Open with the listen, acknowledge, ask, redirect pattern.

scenes.title: I want more money
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I want more money. Response: I hear you. What number were you expecting, and what does that number need to cover? Let the seller explain. Then ask whether price is the only concern or whether timing, condition, costs, and convenience also matter. If the current process may support another written option, collect the facts and have it reviewed before you describe it as available.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea practicing a price objection
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no currency amount or outcome promise.

scenes.title: You give your price first
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: You give your price first. Response: I can explain how the current process reaches a written number. Before that review, I need enough context to avoid giving you a guess. What are you hoping the property can do for you, and what facts should the review account for? Ask about condition, timing, and the seller's priorities. Record the answer, then explain the confirmed next step without inventing a number.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a blank offer-review card
visual_plan.editor_note: The card stays blank and unpromised.

scenes.title: I may list the property
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I am going to list it with an agent. Response: That may be a good path. What would need to be true for listing to work well for you? Ask about preparation, access, timing, expected costs, and the seller's comfort with the process. Do not criticize another path. Help the seller compare what is documented. If professional advice is needed, encourage the seller to get it before deciding.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside two unlabeled sale-path cards
visual_plan.editor_note: Show a neutral comparison without winner graphics.

scenes.title: I am talking with other investors
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I am talking to other investors. Response: That makes sense. What matters most as you compare the options? Ask about the written price, deductions, conditions, timing, and who is responsible for each cost or task. Keep the tone neutral. Another conversation may reveal a better fit. Your job is to make the BMH path clear enough to compare without attacking anyone else or claiming an advantage you cannot verify.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea comparing generic written terms
visual_plan.editor_note: No company logos or rankings.

scenes.title: I need to speak with my spouse
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I will get back to you after I talk to my spouse. Response: That makes sense. What questions do you expect your spouse to have? If it would help, offer a conversation where everyone can hear the same information and ask questions directly. Do not work around a person whose consent matters. Agree on a specific next action that respects the seller's pace and leaves room for the other decision maker.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea arranging a shared conversation
visual_plan.editor_note: Show equal participation and no pressure.

scenes.title: What is your company name
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: What is your company name? Response: We are BMH Group. You should verify who you are dealing with before you rely on anything. Share the current company information and the written materials approved for the conversation. Ask what the seller wants to confirm. If you do not know an answer, record the question and get it from the right source. Trust grows from information the seller can check, not from a confident tone alone.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a generic company-information folder
visual_plan.editor_note: Show BMH Group only, with no outside provider names.

scenes.title: I need more time
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I cannot sell right now. I need more time. Response: I understand. What has to happen before selling becomes workable? Ask about housing, belongings, family coordination, repairs, or another outside event. Then ask how the seller would like you to follow up and what information would be useful then. Do not create urgency that is not real. Let the seller's actual situation define the next conversation.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea listening beside an open-ended next-step card
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no countdown, fixed timeline, or date promise.

scenes.title: Do you have proof of funds
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: Do you have proof of funds? Response: That is a fair thing to ask for. I can provide the current verification material that the process authorizes for this conversation. Tell me what you need to verify and who will review it. Do not send an old document or describe financing beyond what the document proves. If the request needs additional review, say so and follow through with the confirmed material.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a sealed verification folder
visual_plan.editor_note: Show no financial figures, account data, or provider marks.

scenes.title: How did you get my number
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: How did you get my number? Response: Answer with the exact source documented for that contact. Do not guess. Then respect the person's communication preference. If they do not want further contact, follow the current suppression process. If they are open to talking, ask whether the property is something they would consider discussing and what prompted their interest. Privacy and consent come before continuing the script.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a generic contact-source and preference card
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no specific data vendor or phone number.

scenes.title: My attorney needs to review it
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I want my attorney to review it. Response: That is reasonable. Take the time you need to get independent advice. Ask which document the attorney needs and where the seller wants it sent through the approved process. Do not interpret legal language beyond your authority or discourage outside review. Agree on how the seller will raise any questions that come back so the current written terms can be addressed accurately.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea handing over a generic document for independent review
visual_plan.editor_note: Keep the legal review neutral and unhurried.

scenes.title: I will fix it myself
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I will fix it myself. Response: That could be a workable path. What work do you expect to complete, and what would you need in order to finish it? Ask about the seller's plan, access to help, and tolerance for the disruption. Understand the tradeoff the seller is making and let them evaluate the repair plan on its own facts. If they want another option later, leave the door open without predicting what will happen.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a repair-plan folder and an as-is path card
visual_plan.editor_note: Do not imply either path is guaranteed or easier.

scenes.title: I am too busy
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I am too busy. Response: I hear you. Would you rather pause the conversation, choose a better time, or answer a short question that helps me know whether following up makes sense? Respect the answer. If the seller continues, ask what part of the property situation is taking the most attention. Keep the exchange brief and useful. A busy person should not have to fight the script to end the conversation.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea offering pause, reschedule, or continue cards
visual_plan.editor_note: No fixed appointment duration or urgency.

scenes.title: We are taking highest and best
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: We are doing highest and best. Response: Thanks for explaining the process. Ask what must be included, how the seller will compare the written offers, and whether condition, costs, timing, or execution risk matter alongside price. Do not promise to win a bidding process. Submit only what the current review authorizes, and make sure the written terms say exactly what BMH can support.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea reviewing a neutral offer-comparison sheet
visual_plan.editor_note: No winning badge, ranking, or unsupported number.

scenes.title: I have it handled
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I have it taken care of already. Response: Good. What path did you choose, and is there anything still unresolved? The answer may confirm that no follow-up is needed. It may also reveal a timing, condition, or document question the seller still wants help with. Do not manufacture a gap. If the situation is handled, respect that and update the record so the next action matches the seller's answer.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea closing a completed record
visual_plan.editor_note: Show acceptance of the seller's answer.

scenes.title: I may rent it instead
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I am going to rent it instead. Response: That may fit. What would make renting workable for you? Ask about property condition, management, occupants, reserves, and the seller's willingness to handle the ongoing responsibility. Stay within your scope. The goal is to understand the comparison the seller is making. If the seller wants another path reviewed, collect the facts and explain the confirmed next step.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a rental-responsibility card and a sale-path card
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no income figure or earnings claim.

scenes.title: Walk through it before I name a number
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: You go first on the walkthrough, then I will decide. Response: That is fine. I still want to understand what matters to you before the review begins. What condition issues should we expect, what timing are you considering, and what would a workable result need to address? The walkthrough adds property facts. It does not replace the seller's priorities. Record both and avoid naming an unreviewed number.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a property-condition checklist with blank price field
visual_plan.editor_note: Keep the price field blank.

scenes.title: Why do you not buy it yourself
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: If this is such a good deal, why do you not buy it yourself? Response: That is a fair question. Explain only the transaction path and company relationship that the current written process confirms for this property. Do not hide a possible assignment, partnership, or other approved structure. Ask what the seller is concerned might change. Then point back to the written terms, responsibilities, and disclosures that control the agreement.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a transparent transaction-path diagram
visual_plan.editor_note: Use generic parties and no role labels.

scenes.title: Are you wholesaling this
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: Are you wholesaling this? Response: Answer directly using the disclosures and transaction options approved for the situation. If the agreement may be assigned, do not dodge the question. Explain what the seller's written terms say and what could change only through another written agreement. Ask which part worries them: who performs, whether the terms move, or who communicates next. Get any uncertain point reviewed before answering.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside an agreement and disclosure card
visual_plan.editor_note: Show no promised structure or hidden party.

scenes.title: I do not want repairs or inspections
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I do not want to deal with repairs or inspections. Response: Tell me which part you want to avoid. A cash, as-is path may reduce preparation, while access, condition verification, and other terms still depend on the written agreement. Walk through what is confirmed and what remains subject to review. Do not say that nothing can change. Ask the seller to point out any condition issue they already know so the record starts complete.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a property folder marked as-is with open review items
visual_plan.editor_note: Do not show a no-inspection promise.

scenes.title: I do not know what I owe
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I do not know what I owe. Response: That is common. Do not guess at the payoff. Ask what documents or lender information the seller has and explain that the exact amount must come from an authorized statement or closing review. You can still collect the property facts and seller priorities while that amount is being confirmed. Any net discussion waits until the relevant costs and payoff are documented.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside an unopened payoff-request folder
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no debt figure or estimated proceeds.

scenes.title: I have tenants
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I cannot get rid of my tenants. Response: I am sorry this has become difficult. Ask whether there is a written lease, who occupies the property, what notices have been given, and whether any legal process has started. Do not offer legal advice or promise an occupant outcome. Route the facts for qualified review. The seller may also want independent advice before relying on any transaction or occupancy plan.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a sealed occupancy-record folder
visual_plan.editor_note: Avoid eviction imagery or legal-result claims.

scenes.title: I may be underwater
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I owe too much. I may be underwater. Response: Thank you for saying that. We need the exact payoff, any other liens, expected costs, and the written terms before anyone can know what is possible. Collect the documents the current process requires and route the situation for review. Do not promise that a structure will solve the gap. Explain what will be checked and when the seller can expect a confirmed answer.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a document-review path
visual_plan.editor_note: Show no payoff amount or rescue claim.

scenes.title: I am facing foreclosure
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I am in foreclosure and I do not think there is time. Response: I am sorry you are carrying that pressure. Ask for the deadline, the notices received, and the contact information on those documents. Do not promise a legal, lending, title, or credit result. Route the facts immediately through the qualified review path, and tell the seller to seek independent professional advice when the situation calls for it.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea calmly collecting notice details
visual_plan.editor_note: No countdown, rescue claim, or legal outcome.

scenes.title: I need to net my number
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I need to net my number after everything. Response: Understood. What does that amount need to cover, and which costs are you including? Separate the seller's target from facts that still need verification, such as payoff, liens, fees, taxes, or other written obligations. Do not promise proceeds. Once the required facts are documented, the current review can show which written option, if any, addresses the seller's goal.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a blank net worksheet with verified and unverified columns
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no currency figures.

scenes.title: I need to stay after the transaction
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I need to stay in the property after the transaction. Response: Thank you for raising that before anyone relies on a date. Ask how much flexibility the seller needs and what is driving the request. Any post-transaction occupancy requires a written agreement and approval. It may involve conditions, costs, insurance, access, or a holdback that must be reviewed. Document the request without stating that it is available.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a pending occupancy-request folder
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no stay duration or approval badge.

scenes.title: I am worried about a retrade
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I am worried you will change the price after the inspection. Response: I understand why that would worry you. Walk through what the written agreement says about access, condition, review, and any right to request a change or end the agreement. Do not claim that a change can never happen. Ask which past experience or clause created the concern, then get a precise written answer if anything remains unclear.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea reviewing condition language in a generic agreement
visual_plan.editor_note: No certainty badge or price lock claim.

scenes.title: I do not trust terms
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I do not trust creative financing or terms. Response: That is worth slowing down for. Ask what part feels risky or unclear. Explain only the structure that has been reviewed for the property, using the actual written documents. Do not make lending, tax, credit, or legal claims. Encourage independent advice where appropriate. If the seller only wants a cash path, respect that preference and have the available option reviewed on that basis.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a closed terms folder and a separate cash-path card
visual_plan.editor_note: Show no payment, interest, or benefit promise.

scenes.title: Keep this private
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I do not want people knowing my business. Response: I understand. Ask what privacy means to the seller and who may need to be involved for the current process to work. Explain the approved communication and access steps without promising secrecy beyond the written policy and transaction requirements. Record the preference. If the seller wants contact limited to a particular channel, follow the current process for honoring that request.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a private-preference card
visual_plan.editor_note: No public listing image or absolute secrecy claim.

scenes.title: Cash with no contingencies
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I only want cash with no contingencies. Response: Thank you for being clear. Ask what the seller means by no contingencies and which terms matter most. The current review must confirm the price, proof, condition language, title requirements, timing, and every responsibility in the agreement. Do not remove or describe a term before it has been approved in writing. Bring back the exact option the process can support.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a written cash-option checklist
visual_plan.editor_note: Do not display an unconditional badge.

scenes.title: Send your best offer in writing
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: Send your best offer in writing. I do not want to talk. Response: Respect the communication preference. Explain that an accurate written review still needs the property condition, timing, ownership, and seller priorities required by the current process. Ask whether the seller is willing to provide those facts in the preferred channel. If not, document the limitation and do not invent missing information just to send a number.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a generic written-message card and missing-facts checklist
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no phone number, email address, or offer amount.

scenes.title: I need to think about it
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I need to think about it. Response: Of course. What part feels unresolved right now? It may be price, timing, another person, a document, or a question that has not been answered. Listen for the real issue. Offer to provide the confirmed information the seller needs, then agree on a next action that gives them room to decide. Do not turn the request for space into pressure.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea offering an open next-step card
visual_plan.editor_note: Keep the posture relaxed with no urgency.

scenes.title: I am not interested
scenes.spoken_text: Seller: I am not interested. Response: Understood. Before ending, you may ask whether something changed or whether the person wants no further contact, as long as the current process allows that question. Respect the answer immediately. If they explain a concern, listen without arguing. If they want the conversation to end, update the record and follow the suppression or follow-up rule that applies. A clean ending is part of good service.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea closing the conversation respectfully
visual_plan.editor_note: Show a clear stop path and accurate record update.

scenes.title: Know when to pause
scenes.spoken_text: Pause whenever the question crosses into legal advice, lending, credit, taxes, title, an unusual contract term, post-transaction occupancy, or an exception to the current process. Write down the exact question and the facts already confirmed. Tell the seller you are getting it reviewed. Then follow through with the verified answer. A quick unsupported answer creates more risk than an honest pause.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a pause, document, review sequence
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no provider or role labels.

scenes.title: Use the same practice loop
scenes.spoken_text: Run each drill the same way. Hear the seller's words. Acknowledge the concern without pretending you agree with every assumption. Ask a question that reveals the missing fact. Separate what the current documents confirm from what still needs review. Offer the next step that the process supports, then check that the seller understands it. The exact language will change. The judgment behind it should stay steady.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside the reusable practice loop
visual_plan.editor_note: Keep the loop simple and free of script promises.

scenes.title: Continue to the checkpoint
scenes.spoken_text: Take the Objection Scripts Playbook checkpoint next. Then move into Trust, Complexity, and Frequently Asked Questions.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea centered in a medium shot
visual_plan.editor_note: End without viewer-facing chapter numbering.

edit_operations.source_problem: Direct outcome promises are spread across multiple taught comeback beats. The full recut retains all 32 drills and the practice method while replacing promises with conditional discovery and review language.
